source.title: 6. gang Personality Functioning 2026 (Bo Bach)

analysis.theory_role: Presents the new WHO (ICD-11) diagnostic framework for personality disorders, emphasizing a dimensional approach based on the severity of self and interpersonal functioning rather than categorical disease models.
analysis.source_role: Lecture slides introducing the clinical framework of personality functioning and dysfunction, detailing the shift to the ICD-11 model and its practical implications for diagnosis and therapy.
analysis.relation_to_lecture: Serves as the primary visual and structural guide for Lecture 6, outlining the agenda, key definitions, and clinical examples of personality functioning.

central_claims.claim: The new WHO/ICD-11 model for personality disorders shifts from a medical 'disease' model to a psychological model focusing on what it means to be a person (ego-functions).
In personality disorders, problems with self and interpersonal functioning are primary (the source of the problems), whereas in depression and anxiety, these problems are secondary.
Psychotherapy works by strengthening personality functions (formerly ego functions) such as reality testing, impulse control, and self-esteem regulation.
At least 6.9% of the Danish general population recognizes having problems corresponding to a personality disorder, with 4.8% at a mild level.

key_concepts.definition: Psychological functions related to how we handle ourselves (identity, self-worth, goal-directedness) and our relations to others (mentalization, conflict management, cooperation).
key_concepts.role: The core framework for understanding mental health and diagnosing the severity of personality disorders in the ICD-11.

key_concepts.definition: Aspects of the personality including the stability of identity, regulation of self-worth, accuracy of self-appraisal, and goal-directedness.
key_concepts.role: One of the two main axes used to determine the severity of a personality disorder.

key_concepts.definition: The ability to engage in relationships, take others' perspectives, maintain stable mutual relationships, and manage conflicts.
key_concepts.role: The second main axis used to determine the severity of a personality disorder.

key_concepts.definition: The ability to see things from others' perspectives, characterized by curiosity and avoiding overthinking or misunderstanding.
key_concepts.role: A crucial component of interpersonal functioning that is often impaired in individuals with personality dysfunction.

key_concepts.definition: The cognitive ability to perceive reality accurately, which can become distorted under stress (e.g., catastrophizing, black-and-white thinking, transient psychotic symptoms).
key_concepts.role: A key cognitive manifestation of personality functioning that guides behavior and emotional regulation.

distinctions.label: "HOW NUTS?" vs. "WHAT KIND OF NUTS?"
distinctions.summary: Distinguishes between the overall severity of personality functioning ("HOW NUTS") and the specific personality traits or style ("WHAT KIND OF NUTS").
distinctions.stakes: Clarifies the two-step diagnostic process in modern personality psychology: first assessing the severity of dysfunction, then identifying specific trait specifiers.

distinctions.label: Primary vs. Secondary interpersonal problems
distinctions.summary: In personality disorders, self and interpersonal problems are the primary source of the disorder, whereas in depression and anxiety, they are secondary symptoms.
distinctions.stakes: Highlights the unique, foundational nature of personality functioning in psychopathology compared to other mental disorders.

distinctions.label: Medicinsk model vs. Psykologisk model
distinctions.summary: The established medical model treats personality disorders as a "disease", while the new WHO model approaches it as a disturbance in psychological personhood and ego-functions.
distinctions.stakes: Shifts the clinical paradigm towards understanding the patient as a person rather than just treating a categorical disease.

likely_misunderstandings: Students might confuse specific personality traits (the 'what kind') with the overall level of personality functioning (the 'how much/severity').
Students might assume personality disorders are rare, whereas the slides show nearly 7% of the Danish population experiences corresponding problems.
Students might think interpersonal problems are unique to personality disorders, missing the nuance that they are primary in PDs but secondary in anxiety/depression.

quote_targets.target: “HOW NUTS” = SVÆRHEDSGRAD/OVERORDNET FUNKTION / “WHAT KIND OF NUTS” = PERSONLIGHEDSTRÆK
quote_targets.why: Memorably encapsulates the distinction between functioning severity and trait expression using Nancy McWilliams' analogy.

quote_targets.target: Personlighedsforstyrrelse: Selv- og interpersonelle problemer er primære (kilde til problemerne).
quote_targets.why: Defines the core difference between personality disorders and other mental illnesses like depression or anxiety.

quote_targets.target: I dansk almenbefolkning genkender mindst 6.9% at have problemer svarende til en personlighedsforstyrrelse
quote_targets.why: Provides concrete epidemiological data relevant to the Danish context.

grounding_notes: The slides heavily reference the ICD-11 model and WHO guidelines for diagnosing personality disorders.
Clinical examples and quotes regarding psychotherapy and ego functions are drawn from Cabaniss et al. (2010, 2013).
Epidemiological data on the Danish population is cited from Bach et al. (2023).
warnings: The slides contain several visual models and clinical vignettes (e.g., DIPP-11 scoring examples for Grandiose and Vulnerable Narcissism) that lack full context without the accompanying oral lecture.
